text: Forty-year-old female with a past medical history of bleeding gastric ulcer and past surgical history of bilirubin procedure in two thousand thirteen was presented to the clinic with the chief complaints of nausea, vomiting, intermittent diarrhea or constipation and abdominal pain.
Preoperative diagnose recurring gastric ulcer with gastric outlet obstruction.

Preoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed stenosed and ulcerated bilirubin anastomosis and preoperative CT scan revealed dilated stomach of increased size and gastroduodenal stricture causing outlet obstruction.
The patient was brought to the OR with the plan of performing a gastric resection for diagnosis of recurring gastric ulcer with gastric outlet obstruction.
Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon observes severe adhesions, which are dense fibrous bands of tissue connecting abdominal organs.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook and bipolar forceps to carefully dissect and take down massive omental adhesions in the midline, as well as adhesions between the stomach and liver, and between the upper surface of the left lobe of the liver and the diaphragm.
The surgeon performs progressive adhesiolysis between the capsule of the liver and the stomach using a monopolar hook, carefully maintaining the proper surgical plane.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to take down omental adhesions attached to the liver.
The surgeon uses surgical instruments to take down adhesions between the fundus of the stomach and the inferior surface of segment II and III of the liver.
The surgeon mobilizes adhesions between the fundus of the stomach and the diaphragm using a monopolar hook, carefully separating the tissue connections.
The surgeon dissects the greater curvature of the stomach, using a robotic vessel sealer to ligate the short gastric vessels, visibly separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the gastroesplenic ligament using a surgical instrument, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection distally towards the origin of the right gastroepiploic vessels, which are ligated using a hemo-lock clip and robotic vessel sealer.
The surgeon mobilizes the first portion of the duodenum and then transects it using a surgical stapler, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
Following the transection of the duodenum, the surgeon reinforces the staple line with figure-of-eight sutures using PDS-III-O suture material.
The gallbladder appears notably distended, indicating a visible enlargement or swelling.
The surgeon performs a prophylactic cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder to prevent future complications, as endoscopic access to the duodenum will be restricted due to the gastrectomy.
The surgeon divides the pedicle of the left gastric artery while carefully preserving the aberrant left hepatic artery.
The surgeon evaluates the blood perfusion of the proximal gastric stump using indocyanine green (ICG), which highlights the vascular supply to the tissue.
Following confirmation of blood perfusion, the surgeon uses a stapler to divide the stomach, creating a visible staple line across the gastric tissue.
The surgeon prepares the Roux-en-Y jejunal loop by dividing the jejunum with a stapler, followed by constructing a side-to-side enteroenterostomy.
The surgeon closes the root of the Roux-en-Y and the enterotomy using a running suture with PDS-III-O, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.

The surgeon brings the distal jejunal loop into the upper quadrants and performs a gastrojejunal side-to-side anastomosis using a stapler. A small enterotomy is then closed with a running suture of PDS-III-O.
The surgeon resects a short stump of the jejunal loop using a stapler, addressing visible congestion in the tissue.
On completion of the procedure, the patient was extubated in a stable condition to the recovery room.
